docs.title: Scenario templates for community disaster coordination
This is a practical exercise to discuss the potential and challenges in Tribal Nations disaster communication using modern two way text technologies.
docs.text:
National Center for Disaster Preparedness, Columbia University

Funding provided by Twilio

Much of this software was developed by the Wu Lab in the Department of Computer Science and the National Center for Disaster Preparedness in the Climate School, both at Columbia University. The US National Science Foundation funded much of the software infrastructure used.

This is a pilot presentation for the Great Plains Tribal Leaders Health Board 2026 Regional Tribal Emergency Management Summit

Contact: Daniel.Osgood@columbia.edu

docs.title: First phone game test: IKON Colombia
We enjoy coffee
docs.text:
• Gamification interface for WhatsApp

  • Developed at Columbia University with NSF funding, in collaboration with researchers now at the Yale School of the Environment

  • Successfully implemented in the Latin America Coffee sector, http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.3639580

  • Guess which year your neighbors and the satellite would say was worse X or Y?

docs.title: Disaster Preparation Risk Communications are Challenging
Coordination is hard during disasters
docs.text:
• Leadership plays a central role

  • Communities/families/individuals make their decisions and take action

  • Rapid and trusted communication is the most important factor

  • Tribal Nations:
    • Historical reasons for mistrust of government risk comm
    • Language translation challenges
    • Lack of incorporation of Tribal Council viewpoints

docs.title: Standard strategy of sending one way blanket alerts has been useful but has many limits
Town halls and surveys have value, but also leave communication gaps
Modern technology allows for two way phone messaging
Epilogue
docs.text:
This slide will be updated following test run feedback!

There are 3 general scenarios we are considering:

1 Sensitive action communication-eg Evacuate
2 Coordination of actions, resources, and infrastructure-eg Shelter
3

docs.title: Sensitive Issue Safe Space: Evacuation Template
Lets work through the Simulation Template
If this is a live session, please wait for guidance from me when it says wait until the scenario manager instructs you to proceed.
You can use regular text messages or whatsapp
Evacuate Latest Results!
docs.text:
Here are the most recent "live" results from the latest simulation played. This may be the simulation you participated in, if it was the latest!

But be warned--these are dynamic and be broken or simply a systems test.

docs.title: How would you edit this Scenario?
Evacuate Results!
docs.text:
Below are some most recent results from a previous simulation session.

The literature says people with animals are less likely to evacuate, (eg doi.org/10.3390/ani5020173)

Below are some results--what do you see?

Animal owners were less likely to evacuate and renters were most likely to evacuate in that session, reflecting what has been seen in the literature.

For disaster preparedness, actions could be taken, such as providing transportation and shelter that has the capacity to support animals.

What other follow up actions and communications could be done?

docs.title: Simulation Scenarios
docs.text:
• Simulations, tabletops, and role-play are often effective disaster preparedness tools

  • These strategies have been effective in many applications Games 2020, 11(4), 47; https://doi.org/10.3390/g11040047

  • For example, there are many challenges in communication about disaster evacuation

    • Hypothetical Simulations and role-play may provide a safe space for working through challenging topics

docs.title: We are exploring new strategies for disaster preparedness communication possible with two way phone messaging based on scenarios and role play
docs.text:
• Interactive, scenario-based learning

  • Guess what other players would do in scenario

  • Possibilities:

    • Safe space for sensitive issues

    • Move from one-way SMS alerts into…

    • Two-way communication between community and leadership

    • Communication with each other in the community

docs.title: Some Goals and Key Considerations
docs.text:
• Increased preparedness and situational awareness among Tribal Nations

  • Real-time insights for emergency managers

  • Improved trust and engagement with residents

  • Data Sovereignty: Tribes have the inherent right to govern the collection, ownership, access, and use of data for their people, land, and resources

  • Establishing partnerships with Tribal leaderships increase communication, trust, and accountability

  • Incorporating the perspectives of Tribal elders and youth provides connection to culture and community

  • Ensure accessibility across age, literacy, and language

docs.title: Initial steps involve defining templates for families of strategies
docs.text:
• Moving from planning documents to lived decision-making

  • What risk communication “in action” looks like during disasters that strike Tribal Nations

docs.title: Available to be tailored by Tribal Nations or other implimenters as appropriate.
Templates we are currently exploring
docs.text:
1) Sensitive Issue Safe Space: Evacuation

2) Coordination in complex evolving situations: Shelter

3) Resource Stewardship: Stewardship Champions (TBD-need your input!)

docs.title: Columbia University Land Acknowledgement
docs.text: The Lenni-Lenape, Wappinger and Oneida people lived on this land before and during colonization of the Americas. We recognize these Indigenous people of Manhattan, their displacement, dispossession, and continued presence. We are reminded to reflect on our past as we contemplate our way forward to support Indigenous people and other marginalized communities of this land and advance our commitment to justice.

docs.title: IKON Game: Colombia Lessons
docs.text:
• Prize given to best scoring.
  • 146 played, and they invited others
  • Average player played 200 rounds
  • Winner, 16-year-old with her grandmother.
  • Women were more accurate than men
  • Prizes
    • Didn’t improve reporting quality
    • Did increase participation
  • Most people played for fun, not accuracy
  • Everyone might learn…but the information-gathering goal is to identify super trainers, who are about 20% of players who are very accurate--use their responses, understand biases

docs.title: Idea: Make the game configurable by the Emergency or Disaster Manager.
This is particularly challenging in disasters
docs.text:
• Community interaction revealing to everyone

  • Caribbean workshop on events, rainfall, and forecasts

    • Worst storms were the ones that hit on holidays, not necessarily the strongest storms
    • Focus group holiday outcome was verified with research
    • Massive implications for disaster preparedness and planning
  • But we can only do so many focus groups…
  • So, we spent a lot of time and money on technology to help automate
  • Decades of work in Africa, Asia, the Caribbean, and Latin America have yielded new approaches, tools
    • Quantitative hybrid in-person/computerized focus groups became possible at large-scale (many thousands of meetings, millions of people served)

docs.title: Farmers themselves generated better satellite flood and drought models than ever designed before.
IKON Game: Pastoralist Lessons
docs.text:
• WENDOUFI pastoralist game in codesign stages for NASA/USAID SERVIR (unfortunately cancelled)

  • Pastoralists in Senegal need to choose which pond to move to next.

  • NASA products help, need ground training, and skill assessment.

  • Pastoralists must coordinate to prevent congestion.

docs.title: Try it yourself:
docs.text:
Send “noki sn” to +1 (646) 217-0881

#bye to escape

English: “noki senegal english"

docs.title: Shelter Planning Challenges
docs.text:
It is logistically challenging to design and operate disaster shelters, as community members make choices during the chaos of disasters as the situation constantly changes.

Leadership, and Disaster Managers must make complicated decisions. The form below is an example of the kinds of detailed information necessary in this process.

Lets work through the Simulation Template
You can use regular text messages or whatsapp
Shelter Latest Results!
docs.text:
Here are the most recent "live" results from the latest simulation played. This may be the simulation you participated in, if it was the latest!

But be warned--these are dynamic and be broken or simply a systems test.

How well did simulation participants coordinate?

Would it be possible for peopel to coordinate better now that they have this information?

Would there be new coordination challenges to face as the situation evolves?

Text the code the scenario manager gives you to find out!

docs.title: Resource Stewardship: Stewardship Champions
docs.text:
This simulation will be defined following the guidane of the community because it could be highly sensitive or not adequately respect participants if designed without community participation.

The concept is that it is a championship for environmental stewardship

The initial activity would proceed as below, with the actual stewards of the land participating

  • Each participant would report their accomplishments in stewardship practices
  • Each participant would provide their guess at how well they are doing in terms of a measureable metric (eg water quality sample)
  • Participants would be scored based on how well they are able to guess their measureable metric, combined with level of stewardship practices.

What would you suggest?

docs.title: Build your own Evacuate Scenario
docs.text:
The Templates are designed to be tailored by the people who might use them, and to be made available in the appropriate indigenous languages.

This is the form used to tailor the Evacuate Scenario.

Please feel welcome to edit any or all of the text in the boxes to create the code for your own simulation.

For participants who are fluid in indigenous languages, I ask you test the forms to see how well it functions with indigenous languages, and to help us understand what updates might be necessary.

The email is used to identify the code specific to you as a user. If you dont want to share your email, you can enter something else but we wont be able to follow up if you want your simulation activated.

Note that all of the messages that are variables determined by the participants inputs are of course not editable by you, and are in the format %%phoneresponse%%.

docs.title: Build your own Shelter Scenario
docs.text:
The Templates are designed to be tailored by the people who might use them.

This is the form used to tailor the Shelter Scenario.

Please feel edit any or all of the text in the boxes to create the code for your own simulation.

The email is used to identify the code specific to you as a user. If you dont want to share your email, you can enter something else but we wont be able to follow up if you want your simulation activated.

Note that all of the messages that are variables determined by the participants inputs are of course not editable by you, and are in the format %%phoneresponse%%.
